cards.back: Series NMOS. Both must be ON (both inputs HIGH) to pull output LOW. PUN dual: parallel PMOS.
Parallel NMOS. Any ON driver pulls output LOW. PUN dual: series PMOS.
PUN = dual of PDN. Series in PDN ↔ parallel in PUN. Parallel in PDN ↔ series in PUN. This ensures complementary operation (one ON, other OFF).

cards.front: Why is NAND preferred in CMOS?
cards.back: Series NMOS (fast, high μn) is the NAND bottleneck. Series PMOS (slow, low μp) is the NOR bottleneck. NAND is faster because its slow path (PMOS) is parallel.

cards.front: Transistor count for n-input CMOS gate?
cards.back: 2n transistors: n NMOS (PDN) + n PMOS (PUN). A 4-input NAND = 8 transistors.
LOW (0V). Both NMOS ON → PDN connected to GND. Both PMOS OFF → PUN disconnected. Only case with LOW output for 2-input NAND.
HIGH (VDD). Both NMOS OFF → PDN open. Both PMOS ON → PUN connected to VDD. Only case with HIGH output for 2-input NOR.
Zero in all states. PDN and PUN are complementary: when one conducts, the other doesn't. No DC path from VDD to GND ever exists.

cards.front: NMOS-only: NOR preferred. CMOS: NAND preferred. Why?
cards.back: In NMOS-only, speed depends only on pull-down. In CMOS, both networks matter. NOR's series PMOS (slow holes stacked) is the worst bottleneck.

cards.front: How to size series NMOS in CMOS NAND?
cards.back: Each series NMOS should be n× wider than inverter NMOS (n = number of inputs). This compensates for stacked resistance: R_total = R1 + R2 + ... + Rn.
A in series with (B parallel C). PDN implements un-inverted function. AND = series, OR = parallel. PUN dual: A parallel with (B series C).
Output LOW only when ALL inputs HIGH. All other combinations: output HIGH. Y = NOT(A·B). Equivalent to: at least one input LOW → output HIGH.
